title: Needle-felted blackbird
subtitle: A glossy black body with an orange beak in merino
excerpt: Felt a compact blackbird in deep charcoal-black merino with a bright orange beak. The finished bird sits about 7 cm long and works well perched on a wreath, a branch decoration, or as a standalone ornament.
sourceNotes: Synthesised from British needle-felting guild practice. The blackbird is among the most commonly taught UK garden-bird forms in beginner needle-felting workshops.

glossaryTerms.definition: A dry felting method using barbed needles to tangle wool fibres without water. Repeated stabbing with the barbed needle locks the fibres into a firm, sculpted mass.
An internal wire or wire-and-wool skeleton that holds a needle-felted figure upright and gives the limbs their pose before the outer layers of wool are added.

content.text: A male blackbird is one of the most satisfying UK birds to needle felt: the all-black body with the yellow-orange beak reads immediately at a small scale. This version is worked without a wire
, keeping the process accessible for beginners. The bird sits in a perching pose with wings folded close to the body.

attrs.title: Needle safety
attrs.body: Stab straight in and out at 90 degrees into the foam pad only. Wear a finger guard on the supporting hand.

items.name: Merino roving, black or very dark charcoal
Merino roving, pale yellow (for the eye ring)

content.text: Take about 8 g of black merino and fold it repeatedly on itself to form a compact oval about 5 cm long and 3 cm wide. Place it on the foam mat and stab all over with the size-36 needle to compress the core. Work from all sides, turning the core every ten or fifteen stabs. The core should feel firm and hold its oval shape without being squeezed. This takes 5 to 8 minutes of steady work.

Roll about 3 g of black merino into a ball roughly 2.5 cm in diameter. Felt it on the mat to a firm round shape, then position it at the narrower end of the body oval and needle it to join. Work the needle at the join line in short inward strokes to blend the head and body together. The neck should not show as a separate seam.

Pull a thin wisp of black merino about 6 cm long and 3 cm wide. Lay it flat on the mat and needle it into a thin, slightly narrowing tail shape. Attach it to the rear of the body by needling the base firmly into the back surface. The tail should extend flat, not curve up or down.

Roll a small cone of orange merino about 1.5 cm long. Needle it to a firm, slightly flattened cone shape on the mat. Position it at the front of the head with the point facing forward and the base flush with the face, then needle through the base into the head to attach it. Use the size-40 needle for the detail work here; it leaves a cleaner surface than the coarser gauges.
Step 5: Add the eye ring and eyes

A male blackbird has a yellow eye ring. Pull two tiny circles of pale yellow merino (about 3 mm across) and needle each one lightly onto the sides of the head at eye position. Place a tiny black dot at the centre of each ring by stabbing a single black fibre into the yellow circle. Keep the
pressure very light at this stage so the yellow ring does not lose its circular outline.

Cover any uneven areas on the body with a thin final layer of black merino, needling smoothly with the size-40 needle. The surface should be uniform in colour and texture with no pale patches or loose wisps.

items.problem: The head and body look like two separate pieces rather than one bird
items.cause: The join was needled only at the contact point, not blended
items.fix: Add a thin bridge wisp of black merino across the join and needle it in with outward-angled strokes on both sides of the seam. Work the needle into the body on one stroke and into the head on the next, alternating sides.

items.problem: The beak keeps popping away from the head
items.cause: The beak base was not felted flush before attaching, or the base is too small to grip
items.fix: Add a small black merino wisp over the beak base and needle it firmly into the head surface. The merino will lock the beak base in place as it felts into the surrounding fibres.

items.cause: More material was added to one side than the other during body building
items.fix: Add thin wisps of black merino to the flatter side and needle smoothly until both sides match. Rotate the bird and check it from the front before declaring it done.
